added.name: Action Ownership
added.description: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner with a deadline — not a group ask. 'Everyone please review' is a non-action. At the same time, action items should not be over-specified in ways that assume org structure details the email shouldn't contain. The goal is clarity of ownership, not a task breakdown.

added.name: Conciseness and Directness
added.description: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Related points are consolidated rather than fanned out into separate line items. The email should feel like it respects the reader's time.

removed.name: Concrete Specificity
removed.description: Impact, reasoning, and disruptions are named in tangible, specific terms — the actual texture of the situation — rather than in abstract quality or outcome language. 'Dependencies lined up, people informed' over 'undo the work that got us here.'

removed.name: Concise Consolidation
removed.description: Multiple related items resolve into one clear, unified statement rather than fanning out into separate line items. Information is covered efficiently — every section serves a purpose and nothing is over-specified when a consolidated summary does the job.

reworded.name: Emotional Sequencing
reworded.old: The order in which information arrives shapes how the reader receives it. Acknowledgment and context come before the news or ask — they set the frame rather than chasing after it. Each section builds on the emotional state the previous section creates.
reworded.new: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow. This isn't just about being considerate; it's about making the message actually land.

reworded.name: Reader-Centered Framing
reworded.old: The message is framed around what it means for the reader — their situation, their disruptions, their plans — not around the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. When the 'why' names something concrete about the reader's experience, it lands as acknowledgment; when it centers the sender, it reads as self-explanation.
reworded.new: The 'why it matters' is always framed around what it costs or disrupts for the reader — their plans, their team, their timeline — not around the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. 'This matters because your team planned around this date' not 'This matters because we want quality.' Drafts that center the sender's reasoning feel tone-deaf even when technically correct.

reworded.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
reworded.old: Endings land on something that moves people forward — a next step, an invitation, a clear action — rather than trailing into sentiment, gratitude, or motivational language. The closing puts the ball in the reader's court.
reworded.new: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no trailing sentiment. Any gratitude or warmth that comes after the CTA weakens the email. The last thing the reader sees should be something that moves things forward.

reworded.name: Human Voice
reworded.old: The message reads like it's from a person finishing a thought, not from a project tracker, corporate template, or status report. Prose is preferred over bureaucratic structure when it can carry the same information with more warmth and directness.
reworded.new: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template or status report. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.

dimensions_changed.name: Emotional Sequencing
added_dims: There is exactly one concrete next step at the end, not a list of follow-ups
Opening acknowledgment is one sentence max — not a preamble or extended softening
The 'why it matters' section appears before the next step, not after or mixed in
Acknowledgment or situation framing appears before the key news or ask, not after
removed_dims: The opening sets a frame that shapes how the reader receives what follows
Each section flows naturally from the emotional state established by the prior one
Context or acknowledgment appears before the key news or ask, not after

dimensions_changed.name: Reader-Centered Framing
added_dims: Rationale does not center the sender's values, commitments, or organizational priorities
Stakes are described in terms of what the reader is actually dealing with — schedules, dependencies, downstream conversations
removed_dims: Rationale is not framed around the sender's or organization's values or commitments
Impact descriptions reference what the reader is actually experiencing or dealing with, not abstract outcomes

dimensions_changed.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
added_dims: The closing is clean — no padding, no lingering
No additional sentences appear after the call to action
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or 'thanks for your patience' type phrases
The closing is the next step or an invitation — not a sentiment or sign-off phrase
removed_dims: The closing is clean and doesn't linger or add unnecessary padding
The ending puts agency in the reader's hands (e.g., invites questions, opens a door)
No motivational-poster language, generic affirmations, or canned encouragement
The closing moves the reader toward a next step or invitation rather than signing off with sentiment

dimensions_changed.name: Human Voice
added_dims: Sentences have natural, conversational rhythm — not formal or bureaucratic cadence
Tone is direct and neutral — no passive-aggressive undertones
Reads like a person talking to their team, not an institutional communication
Free of corporate jargon and canned phrases
Exclamation marks are used sparingly or not at all
removed_dims: The overall message reads like a person talking to their team, not an institutional communication
Uses flowing prose where bullet lists would make the message feel like a status report
Sentences have natural, conversational rhythm rather than formal or bureaucratic cadence
Avoids corporate communication clichés and canned phrases

reasoning: I made three main changes: (1) I added a new 'Action Ownership' criterion at priority 3 to capture my strong preference for single-owner asks with deadlines over group asks, and my equal dislike of over-specified action items — this was missing entirely from the rubric. (2) I tightened 'Emotional Sequencing' to be more explicit about the strict four-part order and the one-sentence max on acknowledgment, since the sequence itself is the point, not just 'context before news.' (3) I folded 'Concrete Specificity' into the Reader-Centered Framing and Conciseness criteria where it naturally belongs, and replaced it with the Action Ownership criterion which is more distinctly its own thing. I also added a 'nothing_after_cta' dimension to Forward-Momentum Closings since that's a specific failure mode I care about.

reviewed_changes.original_text: You flagged the advanced reporting feature back in the spring, and I know it's been a long wait since then. I wanted to make sure you heard it from me directly: it's shipping next week.
reviewed_changes.new_text: You flagged the advanced reporting feature back in the spring — I wanted to make sure you heard it from me directly: it's shipping next week.
reviewed_changes.reason: Emotional Sequencing (new dimension 'acknowledgment_is_brief': opening acknowledgment must be one sentence max): Two-sentence opener collapsed into one by removing 'and I know it's been a long wait since then' and joining the clauses with an em dash.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I get what it's trying to do, but the em dash version feels abrupt and a little cold. The original two-sentence opener was warm without being bloated — I'd rather trim 'and I know it's been a long wait since then' and just keep it as two short sentences than jam it into one with an em dash that reads awkwardly.

reviewed_changes.original_text: won't require an extra round of formatting before it goes out.
reviewed_changes.new_text: won't require an extra round of formatting.
reviewed_changes.reason: Conciseness and Directness (new dimension 'no_over_explanation': reasoning stated once, cleanly): 'before it goes out' is redundant — the sentence already implies the formatting step is eliminated before sharing.

reviewed_changes.original_text: To turn it on, your IT team will need to enable it through the admin console — it won't activate automatically. Given that it touches your data pipeline, they'll probably want a day or two to review the settings before it goes live.
reviewed_changes.new_text: To turn it on, your IT team will need to enable it through the admin console — it won't activate automatically.
reviewed_changes.reason: Action Ownership (new criterion, dimension 'no_over_specification': action items should not assume internal org details the email shouldn't contain): Removed speculation about how long IT will need and what they'll review — this over-specifies internal process details the sender wouldn't know and the reader doesn't need.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: I can't tell what actually changed here — the original and new text look identical based on what's shown. If nothing changed, this edit shouldn't exist. If something did change that I'm not seeing, I'd need to see the full text to weigh in.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Worth getting a quick call on the calendar so I can walk them through what to expect? Happy to work around their schedule.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Worth getting a quick call on the calendar before next week so I can walk them through what to expect?
reviewed_changes.reason: Forward-Momentum Closings (new dimension 'nothing_after_cta': no additional sentences after the call to action): 'Happy to work around their schedule' is post-CTA sentiment that trails after the closing action. Removed it. Also added 'before next week' to anchor the ask with a concrete timeframe per Action Ownership's 'has_deadline' dimension.

reworded.name: Emotional Sequencing
reworded.old: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first (one or two short sentences), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow. Brevity should come from word choice and trimming, not from merging sentences with punctuation that changes the tone.
reworded.new: Information arrives in a specific order: acknowledge the effort or situation first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters to the reader, then give one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all the pieces are present — breaks the flow.

reworded.name: Reader-Centered Framing
reworded.old: The 'why it matters' is always framed around the reader's concrete reality — what it costs them (bad news) or what it unlocks for them (good news). Their time, their workflows, their team, their downstream conversations — not the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. When this section names something specific and tangible about the reader's situation, it earns whatever ask or action follows.
reworded.new: The 'why it matters' is always framed around the reader's concrete reality — what it costs them or what it unlocks for them. Their time, their workflows, their team, their downstream conversations — not the sender's values, commitments, or organizational rationale. If the 'why' could have been written by someone explaining their own priorities rather than the reader's situation, it's wrong.

reworded.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
reworded.old: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no explanatory reasoning the reader can infer themselves. Once you've made the ask with a deadline, stop. Any sentence after the CTA — whether sentiment or justification — dilutes it.
reworded.new: The closing lands on the next step or an open door — nothing after it. No 'thanks for your patience,' no motivational filler, no trailing sentiment. Once the ask is made, stop. Any sentence after the CTA — whether sentiment or justification — dilutes it.

reworded.name: Action Ownership
reworded.old: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner — a specific named person or the reader themselves — with a concrete deadline. 'Have someone on your IT team' is how things don't get done. Specify WHO and WHEN clearly; don't break the action into sub-tasks or assume process details the email shouldn't contain.
reworded.new: When something needs to happen, there is one clear owner — a specific named person or the reader themselves — with a concrete deadline. Group asks produce no accountability. Specify WHO and WHEN; don't break the action into sub-tasks or assume process details the email shouldn't contain.

reworded.name: Human Voice
reworded.old: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template or status report. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff — the kind of email a sharp colleague would send.
reworded.new: The message reads like a person finishing a thought, not a corporate template. No jargon, no passive-aggressive tone, no excessive exclamation marks. Professional but not stiff.

reworded.name: Conciseness and Directness
reworded.old: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Related points are consolidated rather than fanned out into separate line items. Trusts the reader to connect obvious dots — does not explain reasoning they can infer from context.
reworded.new: Short paragraphs, no walls of text, no over-explanation. Every sentence earns its place. Trusts the reader to connect obvious dots.

dimensions_changed.name: Emotional Sequencing
added_dims: Opening acknowledgment is a single sentence — not two, not merged with an em dash, just one clean sentence
removed_dims: Opening acknowledgment is brief — one or two short sentences — not an extended preamble. Do not merge sentences mechanically (e.g. with an em dash) to hit a sentence count; two short, natural sentences are preferable to one forced one

dimensions_changed.name: Reader-Centered Framing
added_dims: Value or disruption is named with concrete specifics — not abstract benefits or quality statements
removed_dims: Value or disruption is named with concrete specifics — hours saved, people affected, workflows changed — not abstract benefits or quality statements

dimensions_changed.name: Forward-Momentum Closings
added_dims: Does not add explanatory reasoning after the ask that the reader can infer from context
removed_dims: Does not add explanatory reasoning after the ask that the reader can infer from context (e.g., explaining why a deadline matters when the reason is obvious)

added_dims: Action items are not broken into sub-tasks or process steps — specify who and when, not how; do not assume org structure details the email shouldn't contain
removed_dims: Action items are not broken into sub-tasks or process steps — specify who and when, not how

added_dims: Reads like a person talking to a peer, not an institutional communication
removed_dims: Reads like a person talking to their team or peer, not an institutional communication

dimensions_changed.name: Conciseness and Directness
added_dims: Does not explain reasoning the reader can infer from context — treats the reader as an intelligent adult
Related details are consolidated rather than broken into unnecessary bullet points or line items
removed_dims: Related details are consolidated rather than broken into separate bullet points or line items
Does not explain reasoning or justification the reader can infer from context — treats the reader as an intelligent adult

reasoning: Three main changes: (1) Tightened the acknowledgment rule to explicitly say 'one sentence max' — the original allowed two sentences which felt like too much preamble to me. (2) Promoted Forward-Momentum Closings to priority 3 and Action Ownership to priority 4 — trailing sentiment and diffuse asks are pet peeves that were underweighted relative to style concerns. (3) Swapped Human Voice and Conciseness down to 5 and 6 since those are more baseline hygiene for me — the structural and ownership issues are what actually make or break an email.

reviewed_changes.original_text: Your team has been pitching [feature name] to prospects, and I know it's been part of the demo flow. We're pulling it — we found a security issue that makes it unshippable.
reviewed_changes.new_text: Your team has been pitching [feature name] in demos, and we're pulling it — we found a security issue that makes it unshippable.
reviewed_changes.reason: Emotional Sequencing (dimension 'acknowledgment_is_brief' tightened from 'one or two short sentences' to 'a single sentence'): The original was two sentences. Merged into one by folding the context ('pitching in demos') and the news ('we're pulling it') into a single sentence with a natural em dash — keeping the acknowledgment/situation framing while meeting the one-sentence requirement. Also removed 'to prospects, and I know it's been part of the demo flow' as redundant phrasing, replacing with the tighter 'in demos'.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: The merge feels off to me — cramming the acknowledgment and the news into the same sentence loses the beat I need between them. The acknowledgment should land first so the reader feels seen before they get the bad news. When you fold both into one sentence with an em dash, it reads like you're rushing past the acknowledgment to get to the point. I'd keep them as two separate sentences even if that technically violates the 'one sentence' rule — the emotional pacing matters more here.
